name: Search Autocomplete Service
description: Design a search autocomplete system with prefix matching, trie-based caching, and full-text search fallback. Covers prefix indexing, popularity ranking, real-time suggestion serving, and index refresh from the primary database.

learnSteps.title: Trie-Based Prefix Matching
learnSteps.description: The Redis cache stores a trie (prefix tree) as sorted sets: each prefix key maps to a set of completions ranked by popularity score. For query 'sys', Redis returns ZREVRANGE for key 'prefix:sys' yielding ['system design', 'systems programming', 'syslog']. This achieves O(1) Redis lookup + O(K) for top-K results, keeping p99 latency under 5ms. Short prefixes (1-2 chars) are sharded across multiple Redis keys to avoid hot-key issues.

learnSteps.title: Elasticsearch Fallback for Long-Tail Queries
learnSteps.description: When the trie cache misses (rare or long prefixes with <8% miss rate), the service falls back to Elasticsearch using a completion suggester or prefix query. Elasticsearch uses an FST (finite state transducer) index optimized for prefix lookups that returns results in 10-20ms. Results from ES are written back to the Redis trie cache for future requests. This two-tier approach balances speed (Redis for popular) and coverage (ES for long-tail).

learnSteps.title: Popularity Ranking and Freshness
learnSteps.description: Each suggestion has a popularity score derived from search frequency, recency, and trending signals. A batch job runs hourly: it queries PostgreSQL for updated search frequency counts, computes new scores (frequency * recency_decay * trending_boost), and rebuilds the Redis sorted sets. Trending topics get a temporary multiplier that decays over hours. The result is that autocomplete reflects what people are searching for right now, not just historically.

learnSteps.title: Client-Side Optimizations
learnSteps.description: The client implements three key optimizations: (1) Debouncing -- only send a request after 100-150ms of no typing, reducing requests by ~80%. (2) Local caching -- store recent prefix-to-suggestions mappings in a browser/app LRU cache, serving instant results for backspace/retype scenarios. (3) Prefetching -- after receiving results for 'sys', preemptively fetch 'syst' since the user is likely still typing. Combined, these cut server load by 90% while maintaining sub-50ms perceived latency.
